name: HMS P 33 (P 33)
events: The history of HMS P 33 as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine and was compiled in January 2013. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page was last updated in February 2026.
29 May 1941 P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) departed her builders yard at Barrow for Holy Loch. She was escorted by HMS La Capricieuse (Lt.Cdr. G.W. Dobson, RNR). (1)

30 May 1941 P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) arrived at Holy Loch to begin a period of trials and training. She had just completed her acceptance trials and was taken over from the shipyards and placed in full commission upon arrival at Holy Loch. [No logs of HMS P 33 are available for the months of May and June 1941 so details for these months will be missing.] (2)
17 Jun 1941 HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) departed Holy Loch for Gibraltar. She was escorted to Bishops Rock by HMS Cutty Sark (Cdr.(Retd.) R.H. Mack, RN).
[As no log is available for June 1941 no map can be displayed. Also no passage report is available. Details for this passage may thus be missing.] (3)

22 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 1136 A/22, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic Station (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN); Establish patrol near position 43°00'N, 10°40'W. The German vessel Alstertor (3039 GRT, built 1938) was reported in approximate position 37°40'N, 18°00'W at dawn/22 steering north-east at 16 knots. This vessel is thought to be an enemy raider. [The British actually reported her as Alsterufer (2729 GRT, built 1939) by mistake but in fact the vessel was Alstertor.] (4)

23 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 2136 A/23, the Flag Officer Commanding North Atlantic Station (F.O.C.N.A.) sent the following orders to HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN); Raider sunk. Proceed to Gibraltar by original route.

[The Germans had scuttled their ship in position 41°12'N, 13°10'W, when she was intercepted by the destroyers HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.F. de Salis, RN), HMS Fearless (Cdr. A.F. Pugsley, RN), HMS Forester (Lt.Cdr. E.B. Tancock, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Foxhound (Cdr. G.H. Peters, DSC, RN) and HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. T.C. Robinson, RN). (4)
26 Jun 1941 HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) arrived at Gibraltar. She was escorted in by HMS Erin (A/Cdr. J.O. Davies, RNR). (2)
28 Jun 1941 Around 2135 A/28, HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) departed Gibraltar for Malta.
[No passage report is available so details for this passage may be missing.]

For the daily positions of HMS P 33 during part of this passage see the map below. [As there is no log available for June 1941 only positions from 1 July 1941 and onwards can be displayed on the map.]
6 Jul 1941 Around 0730 B/6, HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) arrived at Malta from Gibraltar. (6)

11 Jul 1941 HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 1st war patrol. Her initial orders were to patrol to the north-west of Lampedusa and form a patrol line with HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) and HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) to intercept a southbound convoy but nothing was seen.
HMS P 33 was also to carry out a special operation.

This referred to the convoy made up of the transports Ernesto (Italian, 7399 GRT, built 1914), Nita (Italian, 6803 GRT, built 1913), Castelverde (Italian, 5270 GRT, built 1919), Nirvo (Italian, 5270 GRT, built 1919), Aquitania (Italian, 4971 GRT, built 1924) and the tanker Panuco (Italian, 7751 GRT, built 1941) that were being escorted by the Italian destroyers Fuciliere, Alpino, Lanzerotto Malocello and the Italian torpedo boats Procione, Pegaso and Orsa.

They sailed from Naples at 1930 B/10-2200 B/10 for Tripoli, except Fuciliere and Alpino which sailed from Palermo at 1623 B/11-1630 B/11 and joined the convoy at 2020 B/11, in position 38°28'N, 12°22'E. At 2120 B/12, Panuco had an accidental fire, which was later controlled. Lanzerotto Malocello ordered Orsa to go to her assistance. At 1102 B/13, the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari sailed from Tripoli and, at 1720 B/13 reinforced the escort. As they were close to Tripoli, a heavy fog which limited visibility to 500 metres which delayed their arrival. All ships from the convoy arrived in harbour at 0600 B/14-0810 B/14.
The sailing of the convoy was known from an ULTRA intercept, see file DEFE3/832, pp. 883-884. The destroyer Alpino was not correctly identified but listed as Albona (???). (text garbled).
For the daily and attack positions of HMS P 33 during this patrol see the map below.

12 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 1959 B/12, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta sent the following orders to HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN); At 2100 B/12 proceed to rendezvous in position 180° - Hamamamet lighthouse - 3 miles. Recognition as previously arranged. Boat will be at the rendezvous at 1700 B/14 with one person only on board. Make careful asdic sweep in case position compromised. Whether successful or not leave rendezvous at 2200 B/14 to intercept convoy given in my 2000 B/12 in position 36°27'N, 11°54'E at about 1300 B/15. Convoy is expected at Kerkennah around 0500 hours and and Pantellaria around 1400 hours. After attack or on surfacing after dusk on 15 July return direct to Malta with passengers or to rendezvous repeating identical operation P.M./16.
By 2246 B/12, HMS P 33 had received this entire signal and course was set for the Gulf of Hamamamet. (8)

12 Jul 1941 At 1036 B/12, HMS P 33 received the signal timed 0650 B/12, from the Senior Officer Submarines Malta which stated the following; Six destroyers and six merchant ships are reported in position 37°10'N, 12°00'E on course 180° at a speed of 8 knots.
[For the composition of this convoy see the event for 11 July 1941.] (9)

12 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 2000 B/12 from the Senior Officer Submarines Malta HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) was informed of the following; A 14 knot convoy of six merchant ships and six destroyers is expected to leave Tripoli around 1600 B/14 for Kerkennah and Pantellaria. Convoy may depart from position A or D off Tripoli.

This signal must have referred to the convoy made up of the transports Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927), Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6338 GRT, built 1939), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940), Barbarigo (Italian, 5293 GRT, built 1930) and Ankara (German, 4768 GRT, built 1937) escorted by the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello, Alpino, Fuciliere and the Italian torpedo boats Procione, Pegaso and Orsa.

The sailing of the convoy was know due to an ULTRA decrypt (see DEFE3/832, page 830A). Five (not six) merchant ships were correctly identified. The escorts to be provided by the six which had arrived with the Ernesto convoy. They sailed from Tripoli at 1430 B/14-1700 B/14 and arrived at Naples at 1410 B/16-1449 B/16 (except Alpino and Fuciliere which were detached to Palermo where they arrived at 0400 B/16-0408 B/16 and Barbarigo which was sunk on the 15th). (20) (8)

12 Jul 1941 In a signal timed 0915 B/12, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta gave the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. E.A. Woodward, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN). Cancel patrol line ordered. HMS Unbeaten patrol in position 35°40'N, 11°55'E and HMS P 33 patrol in position 35°50'N, 12°08'E until in contact with the enemy. Neither submarine is to cross 35°45'N. Final enemy report will be passed on the next Rugby operational routine. If speed allows HMS Unbeaten should also shadow and attack convoy during the night and then operate in area 'X'. (8)

14 Jul 1941 At 1712 B/14, HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) received the signal timed 1551 B/14 from the Senior Officer Submarines Malta which contained the following orders; My 1959 B/12. Special operation postponed till Wednesday and/or Thursday with identical arrangements except possibly two 'refugees' in the boat. Proceed now to intercept the convoy reported in my 2000 B/12 returning direct to Malta on completion of the special operation. (9)

15 Jul 1941 HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) attacked a convoy and torpedoed and sank the Italian merchant Barbarigo (5293 GRT, built 1930) off Pantellaria Island in position 36°27'N, 11°54'E.
[For the composition and further details of this convoy see one of the events for 12 July 1941.]

Following the attack the submarine had been sighted by a CANT Z.501/6 of 144^ Squadriglia and attacked with two bombs. The destroyer Fuciliere had seen the torpedo tracks and dropped 28 depth charges, followed by Alpino who dropped more depth charges. The destroyers rejoined the convoy and Pegaso was ordered to pick up the survivors while Procione and Orsa were detached to hunt the submarine while CANT Z.501/2 of 144^ Squadriglia also joined the hunt.
1407 B/15 - The convoy P 33 was warned about was sighted in position 36°27'N, 11°54'E.

1416 B/15 - It was noticed that the convoy was made up of five half laden merchants. These were in two columns of two ships each in line ahead with one more ship leading in the middle of the two columns. The escort was identified as six Spica class torpedo boats in pairs of two, one pair ahead and one pair on each quarter of the convoy. The escorts were under constant wheel. Also an aircraft was overhead. P 33 was at 11000 yards and closed to attack the leading ship of the starboard column. The size of this ship was estimated at 7000 tons.

1439 B/15 - Four torpedoes were fired at the intended target from 2500 yards. 2 hits were heard about 2 minutes after firing. Almost immediately a heavy counter attack began that lasted until 1605 hours, in all 116 depth charges were dropped but only one pattern fell close knocking out some lights. After the attack on the merchant Lt. Whiteway-Wilkinson took P 33 to 70 feet but she lost her trim and control was only regained at 310 feet. Due to this extreme depth several leaks occurred and the pressure hull was distorted. This forced Lt. Whiteway-Wilkinson to abandon the patrol and head to Malta for repairs. This damage was not due to the depth charging but due to the great depth that P 33 ended up after losing her trim. (7)
16 Jul 1941 At 1230 B/16, HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Malta. (7)
21 Jul 1941 HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) was docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard for repairs to her pressure hull. (6)
28 Jul 1941 HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) was undocked. (6)
6 Aug 1941 HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 2nd war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in area 'T', to the west off Tripoli, Libya. (10)

12 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1121 B/12, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta, informed HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) of the following; HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) operates on coast east of 013°30'E and south of 33°10'N, from A.M./14. (11)

14 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 0953 B/14, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) of the following; Position, course and speed of three enemy merchant vessels and two schooners 34°01'N, 11°57'E, 190°, 5 knots.

These were the Italian steamer Sirena (974 GRT, built 1883), the Italian schooners V 72 / Fratelli Garré (413 GRT, built 1920) and V 113 / Francesco Garré (395 GRT, built 1917) escorted by the Italian gunboat Grazioli Lante (295 tons, built 1912), the Italian naval auxiliary Sivigliano (1270 GRT, built 1921) and the Italian auxiliary schooner M 9 / Concetta Falco (152 GRT, built 1920). They had sailed from Lampedusa at 1530 B/13 and arrived at Tripoli at 1830 B/15. (11)

14 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1601 B/14, HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) were given the following orders; HMS P 32 was to patrol in position 33°10'N, 13°14'E and HMS P 33 in position 33°20'N, 13°00'E. A 12.5 knots convoy made up of six merchant ships and five destroyers due Tripoli 1300 B/15 and an 8 knots convoy made up of four merchant ships and six destroyers due Tripoli 1630 B/18.

The first convoy referred to was the one made up of the transports Andrea Gritti (Italian, 6338 GRT, built 1939), Francesco Barbaro (Italian, 6343 GRT, built 1940), Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927), Sebastiano Venier (Italian, 6311 GRT, built 1940) and Vettor Pisani (Italian, 6339 GRT, built 1939). This convoy had departed Naples around 1330 B/13-1700 B/13 at 13.5 knots bound for Tripoli. The convoy was escorted by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi (escort leader), Folgore, Strale, Lanzerotto Malocello, Fulmine and the Italian torpedo boat Orsa. At 1835 B/13, in position 167° - Punta Carena - 8.5 miles, a reconnaissance aircraft dropped a white smoke flare and signalled a submarine ahead of Ugolino Vivaldi, followed by two bombs. The destroyer dropped four 100-kg depth charges, three set at 100 metres, and one set at 75 metres, and two 50-kg depth charges set at 50 metres, the torpedo boat Orsa also dropped a number of depth charges. As the Vivaldi forward gun turret opened fire to signal the presence of the submarine to the convoy, a loading accident occurred as the second salvo was fired, wounding thirteen of the gun crew, four of them seriously. At 1905 B/13, Ugolino Vivaldi ordered Orsa to rejoin the convoy and Vivaldi herself turned back to land her wounded in Naples at 2200 B/13, with Folgore now assuming the escort command. There were no enemy submarines in the area, the sighting was 'bogus' as was often the case. The convoy was attacked by enemy bombers at 0105 B/15, 25 miles SW of Lampdedusa. At 1315 B/15-14150 B/15, it arrived at Tripoli without further incident.

The second convoy referred to was the one made up of the transports Caffaro (Italian, 6476 GRT, built 1924), Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1926), Maddalena Odero (Italian, 5545 GRT, built 1921), Marin Sanudo (Italian, 5958 GRT, built 1926), Nicolò Odero (Italian, 6003 GRT, built 1925) and the tanker Minatitland (Italian, 7651 GRT, built 1941). This was the 40th Seetransport Staffel which had departed Naples around 0030 B/16. It was escorted by the Italian destroyers Freccia, Euro, Dardo and the Italian torpedo boats Procione and Pegaso. The torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Palermo at 1600 B/16 to reinforce the escort. Off Trapani, Procione was detached and entered this port at 1300 B/28. Eight miles south of Lampedusa, Maddalena Odero was torpedoed twice by RAF bombers at 2045 B/17. Pegaso towed her to Lampedusa while Sirtori looked for survivors. Giuseppe Sirtori, Pegaso and the stricken vessel entered this port at 1215 B/18 (or 1020 B/18, sources give different times). At 1300 B/18, Maddalena Odero was hit by a bomb from a RAF aircraft and blew up. Pegaso turned back, arriving at Trapani at 0625 B/19, while Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Lampedusa at 2015 B/18 for Porto Empedocle. The remainder of the convoy reached Tripoli at 1700 B/18-1800 B/18.

This convoy had also been attacked in position 39°35'N, 13°18'E by HrMs O 23 (Lt.Cdr. G.B.M. van Erkel, RNN) at 1004 A/16 (1115 B/16 according to the Italians) from long range but all torpedoes missed. The torpedoes were sighted by Freccia and by a CANT Z.501 seaplane. (11)

15 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1144 B/15, the Cdr. S.1 transmitted the following orders to HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN); My 1601 B/14. At 2100 B/18 unless the second convoy referred to is sighted before then HMS P 32 resume patrol in area V, HMS Unique will proceed to Area T and P 33 proceed direct to position 35°30'N, 14°10'E to arrive at Marsamxett [Malta] before 2030 B/19 or after 0630 B/20.
In a signal timed 2005 B/15, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta ordered HMS P 33 to amend her date of arrival to 20th or 21st. (11)

17 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 2015 B/17, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Unique (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN), HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HMS P 33 (Lt. R.D. Whiteway-Wilkinson, DSC, RN) of the following; Second convoy (referred to in the signal timed 1601 B/14) due Tripoli about noon/18. When passed HMS P 33 return to Malta as in my signal timed 1144 B/15. HMS Unique and HMS P 32 proceed to patrol in the vicinity of positions 33°20'N, 13°03'E and 33°17'N, 13°14'E respectively to intercept three large motor ships and six destroyers. Enemy speed speed 17.5 knots. Enemy due Tripoli about noon/20.

The fast convoy referred to was the convoy made up of the troop transports (liners) Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912), Neptunia (19328 GRT, built 1932), Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920). Their escort was made up of the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Nicoloso Da Recco, Alfredo Oriani and Vincenzo Gioberti. (the Italian torpedo boat Orione could not join them due to engine defects). This convoy sailed from Naples at 2250 B/18-0140 B/19.

This convoy was reinforced by the torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza (sailed from Trapani at 0910 B/19 until Tripoli) off Cape San Vito at 1330 B/19 and at 1450 B/19 from Marettimo by the destroyers Maestrale, Grecale and Scirocco (the destroyers sailed from Palermo at 0730 B/19, the first two escorted the convoy until 0100 B/20 and returned to Palermo, while the third went on to Tripoli).
This convoy reached Tripoli at 1230-1415 B/20. (11)
ADM numbers indicate documents at the British National Archives at Kew, London.
